vitals.field_size: 552 riders, 15 countries, 20 US states (2023 inaugural edition — no independently confirmed total for 2024 or 2025)

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 60. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 59.

course_description.character: GFNY Maryland runs the standard GFNY two-tier format on Dorchester County's flat Eastern Shore marsh-and-farmland terrain: a 92.4-mile Long Course (481 ft climbing) that is fully competitive with overall and age-group classification, and a 48-mile Medium Course (254 ft) that receives a finish time but is explicitly non-competitive, published alphabetically rather than ranked. GFNY itself describes the route as 'flat and fast,' with wind rather than elevation as the real variable. Long Course riders repeat a loop out of downtown Cambridge. Inaugurated October 1, 2023, the race has run three editions (2023-2025); 2026 will be its fourth.
course_description.signature_challenge: Holding position in a large, fast, flat group where breaks rarely stick — per rider accounts, the race stays bunched for long stretches and positioning/patience matter more than raw power on this terrain.
suffering_zones: The flat, fast opening miles where a big group locks in together and few breakaways survive
Open, wind-exposed Eastern Shore farmland and marsh roads — wind, not elevation, is the course's real difficulty

The closing miles of the Long Course, where riders in contention for an age-group qualifying placement (GFNY's qualification is placement-based within category, not a fixed time standard) have to commit fully rather than coast to the line

biased_opinion.verdict: Legitimate GFNY Stop, Honest About What the Medium Course Actually Is
biased_opinion.summary: GFNY Maryland is a real, still-young addition to the GFNY U.S. calendar — inaugurated October 1, 2023 with 552 riders from 15 countries and 20 states, and confirmed again for October 4, 2026. It runs GFNY's standard two-tier format on Dorchester County's flat Eastern Shore terrain: the 92.4-mile Long Course carries genuine age-group competition, while the 48-mile Medium Course gets a finish time but no ranking. Our prior elevation figure (1,200 ft) overstated the course by roughly 2.5x — official and municipal documentation puts the Long Course at 481 ft, consistent with rider reports describing the race as 'very flat' with a big, hard-to-break group. As with other GFNY stops, wind is the real obstacle here, not terrain.
strengths: GFNY's standard competitive structure applies on the Long Course, with age-group placement stakes
Flat course rewards positioning and group tactics as much as pure power
552 riders from 15 countries and 20 states at the 2023 debut — a real number, not an organizer estimate
Flat Eastern Shore terrain and a compact Cambridge base keep the course itself simple to navigate, though we have no independent data on broader event logistics (parking, lodging supply, etc.)
weaknesses: Only three editions deep (2023-2025) — a short track record next to GFNY's longer-running franchise stops
The 48-mile Medium Course pays a finish time but is explicitly non-competitive — alphabetical results only, no ranking, even though GFNY markets it as a race distance
Flat, low-elevation terrain (481 ft over 92.4 miles) means minimal scenic or physical variety compared to GFNY's mountain fondos — wind exposure is the main variable, and it's unpredictable
No independently confirmed field-size figure exists for 2024 or 2025, so year-over-year growth can't be verified from public sources
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A structurally sound GFNY stop for riders who want age-group competitive stakes on flat terrain. Choose the Long Course if you actually want to race; the Medium Course is a supported ride with a clock, not a result.

final_verdict.one_liner: Fast, flat GFNY qualifier on the Chesapeake's Eastern Shore — real racing on the Long Course, a supported ride on the Medium.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes for the Long Course if you want genuine GFNY age-group competition on flat, fast terrain. Choose the Medium Course only if you're fine with a non-competitive, timed-but-unranked ride.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a comparable flat, wind-exposed GFNY stop in the U.S.: GFNY Florida (now relocated to Hardee County for 2026). For genuine climbing instead of flat-terrain racing: GFNY Grand Ballon (France).

videos.title: Part 1, GFNY Cambridge: how the race unfolded and my strategy.
videos.transcript:
hello from Florida I want to give you a
recap of the race on Sunday this was the
gfny Cambridge Maryland race and it was
very flat It was 95 mil about and maybe
400 ft or less of elevation gain so this
is going to be a two-part series I'm
going to talk about the first half of
the race today and then check out
tomorrow's video for the second half of
the race and I use different strategies
a little bit for each part okay so I'm
going to walk you through
chronologically I was at the front of
the race I had number three lucky number
three and so that was a great spot
because basically I didn't have to make
up anytime I just had to maintain my
position so basically I just went with
the first people and I tried to stay up
with them at the start it was a very
fast start and I just wanted to be with
those first people just in case there
was a break which there ended up not
being one eventually everyone just kind
of grouped together and it became about
a hundred or more people just going down
the road so this was a very big group
and I was just kind of watching the
Dynamics unfold every so often there'd
be an attack off the front of a couple
people or even the group would split a
little bit but eventually it would
always come back together so I realized
that people at the front were strong
enough and willing to pull that it was
going to be very very hard for a break
to form also I was watching the women
the women were not going with any of the
breaks and I'm not racing the men I'm
racing the women so I just want to be
with them I didn't try to go with any of
the brakes because I concluded that
those brakes probably weren't going to
stick and if they did stick it's okay
because no women were there and so I was
just slotted into pretty much the middle
of this big group sometimes towards the
front and I didn't really care if I was
you know if I drifted back a bit
sometimes i' move up I didn't care if
the other women were in front of me
because for the first half of this race
it was just sit in and be comfortable
the name of the game was be patient no
actions happening just sit in and wait
till the second half of the race where
things might start happening and that's
where the video is going to pick up
tomorrow

quotes.text: It was very flat, about 95 miles with maybe 400 ft or less of elevation gain. It was a very fast start and eventually everyone just kind of grouped together and it became about a hundred or more people just going down the road.

Every so often there'd be an attack off the front of a couple people or even the group would split a little bit but eventually it would always come back together. People at the front were strong enough and willing to pull that it was going to be very very hard for a break to form.

terrain_notes.text: Very flat course with only about 400 feet or less of elevation gain over 95 miles according to rider experience.

race_day_tips.text: The race starts very fast so positioning at the front early is crucial to avoid having to make up time later.
For the first half of the race, focus on sitting in the group and being patient - save energy and wait for actions to happen in the second half.
Expect a very large group of 100 or more riders staying together for much of the race, making breaks difficult to establish.

additional_quotes.text: I was at the front of the race I had number three lucky number three and so that was a great spot because basically I didn't have to make up anytime I just had to maintain my position
People at the front were strong enough and willing to pull that it was going to be very very hard for a break to form

rider_intel.search_text: GFNY Maryland Cambridge is described by riders as a very flat 95-mile course with minimal elevation gain of approximately 400 feet or less. The race features a very fast start with riders needing to position themselves at the front early to avoid making up time later. A large group of 100 or more riders typically stays together for much of the race, making breakaways difficult to establish due to strong riders at the front willing to pull the group back together. Race strategy centers around patience in the first half, sitting in the large group comfortably while saving energy for potential actions in the second half of the race. The flat terrain and group dynamics create a race where positioning and timing matter more than climbing ability.

eligibility.notes: 2026 edition confirmed for October 4 in Cambridge, Maryland via both the official GFNY site and the City of Cambridge's 2026 course-operations document. Corrected elevation from a previously overstated 1,200 ft to the officially/municipally documented 481 ft (Long Course). Founded 2023 confirmed correct (announced Dec 2022, first edition Oct 1 2023); three editions run through 2025, 2026 will be the fourth.
